email.name: Email integration (Thunderbird bridge)
email.description: How work-buddy reads email via the thunderbird-work-buddy companion extension and feeds it into the triage Review pool

content.full:
## Architecture

```
work-buddy agents
   ‚îÇ  MCP
   ‚ñº
work-buddy capabilities (email_*)
   ‚îÇ  EmailProvider protocol
   ‚ñº
ThunderbirdEmailProvider (HTTP client)
   ‚îÇ  Authorization: Bearer ‚Ä¶  on 127.0.0.1:<dynamic_port>
   ‚ñº
thunderbird-work-buddy companion extension (separate repo)
   ‚îÇ
   ‚ñº
Thunderbird Mail API
```

The companion extension is **vendored separately** at `KadenMc/thunderbird-work-buddy`. It does nothing on its own ‚Äî it exposes a narrow, authenticated, read-only HTTP bridge so work-buddy Python can read mail data. The agent-facing surface lives entirely inside this repo as MCP capabilities.

## Bridge surface (read-only v1)

The extension exposes a small REST API on a localhost port discovered via a connection file at `<tmpdir>/thunderbird-work-buddy/connection.json`:

| Method | Path                | Use |
|--------|---------------------|-----|
| GET    | `/health`           | liveness probe + plugin metadata |
| GET    | `/accounts`         | enumerate accounts; default-deny allowlist |
| POST   | `/folders`          | folder tree under an account |
| POST   | `/messages/recent`  | recent unread/all summaries |
| POST   | `/messages/search`  | token search across header fields |
| POST   | `/messages/get`     | fetch one message including body |
| POST   | `/messages/display` | open a message in Thunderbird UI |
| POST   | `/messages/exists`  | quarantine probe ‚Äî "is this still at this folder URI?" |

No compose / send / move / delete / contacts / calendar in v1. Adding any of those requires extension-side trust changes plus capability-side `@requires_consent` gating.

## Provider abstraction

`work_buddy.email.provider.EmailProvider` is a Protocol; backends live under `work_buddy.email.providers.*`. The factory `get_email_provider()` reads `email.provider` from config (default: `"thunderbird"`). `"fake"` selects an in-memory provider used by tests and dry runs. This abstraction keeps the door open for Gmail API / Microsoft Graph / IMAP backends without touching capabilities or the triage adapter.

Key provider methods:
- `recent_messages` / `search_messages` ‚Äî header summaries
- `get_message` ‚Äî body fetch
- `display_message` ‚Äî open in UI
- `message_exists` ‚Äî three-state liveness probe (`True` / `False` / `None` for ambiguous, used by the source-removed quarantine trigger)

## Stable keys vs operational handles

- **Stable key** ‚Äî derived from RFC 822 Message-ID where available, hash of `(from, date, subject)` otherwise. Survives Thunderbird restarts and folder moves. This is the durable identifier we put on `TriageItem.id` (after hashing) and key dedup off of.
- **Operational handle** (`EmailMessageHandle`) ‚Äî the bridge's transient id (Thunderbird's `messageId`) plus the folder URI. Used for follow-up calls (display, get-body, exists). Carried in `TriageItem.metadata` rather than the id.

Gmail's labels-as-folders model surfaces the same RFC Message-ID under multiple folder URIs (INBOX, [Gmail]/All Mail, [Gmail]/Important). The triage adapter dedups by stable_key within a single run, preferring inbox > archive > junk > trash via `_FOLDER_TYPE_PRIORITY`.

## Default-deny account access

The extension's options page presents a checkbox per account; an empty allow-list means **no accounts exposed** (intentionally different from upstream `TKasperczyk/thunderbird-mcp`'s "empty = all"). The `email_accounts` capability shows the user which accounts are currently visible.

## Triage integration

`email_triage_run` is the cron-callable entry point: collect recent messages, dedupe, drop into the triage Review pool with `source="email_message"`.

### Verdict-pass modes (Slice 2)

The LLM verdict pass is gated by `triage.verdict_pass.enabled` (and per-source override `triage.verdict_pass.sources.email.enabled`). Two modes:

- **Off** (default): items land as raw captures (`verdict={"raw": true}`) for human review. Slice 1 behavior preserved.
- **On**: `LLMRunner` per item, structured verdict via `VERDICT_SCHEMA`, mapped into the existing `TRIAGE_ACTIONS` (`close`/`create_task`/`record_into_task`/`leave`/`group`). The agent prompt (`_AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT` in `email/capabilities.py`) is biased toward `close` for community events / mailing-list invites / automated notices ‚Äî its core question is "would anything materially break if the user never read this?".

  - Default tier: `FRONTIER_BALANCED`; `local_fast` works for most messages but Qwen's 4096-token window is tight at full body cap.
  - `include_body_chars` auto-bumps to `_DEFAULT_VERDICT_BODY_CHARS` (800) when verdict pass is on; 0 (headers only) when off. Empirically tuned ‚Äî bigger and you trip context-overflow on long emails.
  - When `include_body_chars` overruns context, the LM Studio adapter classifies the error as `context_exceeded` (matched on `n_keep`/`n_ctx` tokens), and `call_for_verdict` auto-escalates to a larger-context tier.

### Per-source verdict-pass gating

`work_buddy.triage.config.is_verdict_pass_enabled_for(cfg, source)` resolves the gate via:

1. `triage.verdict_pass.sources.<source>.enabled` (per-source override; explicit true OR false wins)
2. `triage.verdict_pass.enabled` (global default)
3. `False` (default)

Known source keys (`KNOWN_VERDICT_PASS_SOURCES`): `journal`, `inline`, `email`, `web_browser` (Chrome ‚Äî reserved for a future capability, key sits there pre-staged).

### Source-removed quarantine

The `email_message` source descriptor lists `TRIGGER_SOURCE_REMOVED` in its `quarantine_triggers`. The trigger calls `provider.message_exists(handle)` per pending entry on each `triage_pool_sweep` pass. When the bridge says `False`, the entry quarantines with reason `source_removed`. Bridge ambiguity (unreachable, account access changed, malformed handle) returns `None` and **never** quarantines ‚Äî the unattended sweep must not punish a brief outage.

### Card actions seam

The `email_message` source descriptor declares an `open_action`:

```python
"open_action": {
    "label": "Open in Thunderbird",
    "capability": "email_display",
    "param_map": {
        "provider_message_id": "metadata.provider_message_id",
        "folder_path":         "metadata.folder_path",
    },
    "quarantine_on_error_kinds": ["email_message_not_found"],
}
```

`work_buddy.triage.card_actions.build_card_actions` reads this from any source's descriptor and emits an `actions` array on each Review card. The dashboard renders one button per action; click POSTs to `/api/palette/execute`. When the action fails with one of the declared `quarantine_on_error_kinds`, the dashboard auto-fires `triage_pool_quarantine_entry` so the stale card vanishes without waiting for the cron sweep.

The presentation builder also drops the legacy `url` field for items whose source has `open_action` ‚Äî handles dead `thunderbird:msg/<key>` synthetic links on pre-Slice-2 pool data.

## Why not extend TRIAGE_ACTIONS

Email-specific actions like archive / unsubscribe / reply don't currently fit cleanly into `(close, group, create_task, record_into_task, leave)`. Rather than reshape the core schema before the loop is proven, the verdict pass maps email LLM verdicts conservatively into existing actions (newsletter/event invite ‚Üí `close`, action-required ‚Üí `create_task`, ambiguous ‚Üí `leave`). Source-specific verdicts can be added once the dashboard renders email cards distinctly.

## Setup

1. Clone and install the companion extension from `KadenMc/thunderbird-work-buddy`.
2. In the extension's options, tick the accounts you want exposed.
3. In `config.local.yaml`: `tools.thunderbird.enabled: true`.
4. (Optional) Enable verdict pass: `triage.verdict_pass.sources.email.enabled: true`.
5. Restart Thunderbird and the work-buddy MCP gateway.
6. `wb_run('email_health')` ‚Äî should report `ok: true`.

When Thunderbird is closed or the bridge is unreachable, the `thunderbird` tool probe fails and the `email_*` capabilities are filtered out of the live registry ‚Äî `wb_run('feature_status')` shows them under disabled-capabilities with the bridge as the missing dependency.

## Capabilities

- `email_health` ‚Äî bridge liveness probe.
- `email_accounts` ‚Äî list bridge-visible accounts.
- `email_triage_run` ‚Äî one BackgroundTriageProducer pass; idempotent. Honors per-source verdict_pass gate; accepts `tier` override; auto-picks `include_body_chars` based on mode.
- `email_get` ‚Äî fetch one message body via the operational handle.
- `email_display` ‚Äî open a message in Thunderbird's UI (3pane / tab / window).

All five carry `requires=['thunderbird']` so the registry filters them out when the bridge is unreachable.

## Related

- `triage/card-actions` ‚Äî the per-source open-in-app action seam used by Review cards.
- `triage/source-descriptors` ‚Äî lifecycle TTLs + quarantine trigger registry.

email.dev_notes: **Don't reuse the `tools.obsidian.bridge_port` strategy here.** The Thunderbird bridge dynamically picks a port and writes a connection file at `<tmpdir>/thunderbird-work-buddy/connection.json`; the Python client discovers it. A fixed port collides badly when users run multiple Thunderbird profiles. **Don't add mutating routes to the extension without consent gates.** The v1 read-only surface is asserted by `tests/routes.test.cjs` in the extension repo. Any compose / move / delete capability needs `@requires_consent` on the Python side AND an explicit feature flag in the extension's options page. **The `thunderbird` tool probe is intentionally cheap.** It does a TCP probe + one authenticated `/health` round-trip; do not extend it to walk folders or fetch messages. **Verdict pass body-cap interaction with local models.** `_DEFAULT_VERDICT_BODY_CHARS=800` was tuned empirically against Qwen 14B's 4096-token window plus the system prompt + active-tasks context block. Bumping the cap requires either a larger-context model or trimming the prompt. The classifier fix (work_buddy/llm/runner_v2.py + backends/_errors.py 2026-04) means context-exceeded errors auto-escalate now, but you'll burn cycles on a wasted local call before the escalation fires.

email/triage-directions.name: Email triage directions
email/triage-directions.description: Run one BackgroundTriageProducer pass over recent email and drop new items into the Review pool

content.full:
## Goal

Run a single email-triage pass ‚Äî collect recent messages from the configured email provider (Thunderbird bridge by default), dedupe against the existing pool, and drop new captures into the triage Review pool with `source="email_message"`. The user reviews them in the dashboard like any other triage card.

## Steps

1. **Probe.** Call `email_health` first. If it returns `ok: false`, surface the `error_kind` to the user ‚Äî the most common cause is the bridge being down or no accounts being allowed in the extension's options.

2. **Account-allow check.** If `email_health` is OK but `accessible_accounts == 0`, tell the user to open Thunderbird ‚Üí Add-ons ‚Üí Work Buddy Bridge options and tick at least one account. Do not silently produce zero candidates.

3. **Run.** Call `email_triage_run` with the user's preferred window:
   - default: `days_back=2, max_messages=50, unread_only=true`
   - if the user asks for "all recent mail" relax `unread_only` to false but cap `days_back` to 7
   - if `dry_run=true` was passed, the pool is not written

4. **Report.** The result dict has `status` (`ok` / `skipped` / `error`), `submitted` count, and a list of un-submitted item IDs with reasons. Prefer numeric summaries over dumping the items dict.

## What you don't do

- **Don't compose / reply / forward / send.** Not exposed in v1. Tell the user to open the message in Thunderbird (`email_display`) and reply there.
- **Don't extend `TRIAGE_ACTIONS`.** Email-specific verdicts (archive, unsubscribe) are deferred until the dashboard renders email cards distinctly.
- **Don't bypass the triage substrate.** If the user says "check my email" without specifying triage, ask whether they want a manual scan (one-shot via this capability) or recurring triage (the cron job under `sidecar_jobs/`).

## Failure modes

| `error_kind`                  | Meaning                                                   | Action |
|------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|--------|
| `email_provider_disabled`    | `email.enabled: false` in config                          | Tell user; suggest config flip |
| `email_bridge_unreachable`   | Thunderbird closed or extension not running               | User must open TB / install ext |
| `email_bridge_unauthorized`  | Stale connection-file token (race with TB restart)        | Restart TB to refresh token |
| `email_message_not_found`    | Follow-up call for a moved/deleted message                | Surface, don't retry |
| `email_provider_error`       | Generic 4xx/5xx from the bridge                           | Surface verbatim |

email/triage-directions.dev_notes: **Slice 1 has the verdict pass disabled.** Items land in the pool with `verdict={"raw": true}`. If somebody flips `verdict_pass_enabled=True` on the producer, the agent stub raises ‚Äî that's intentional surfacing of a misconfiguration, not a bug. The proper fix is to wire a real LLM agent like `journal_triage_scan` does, which we've deferred until the dashboard grows email-card rendering.
email/triage-directions.trigger: When the user invokes /wb-email-triage or asks to triage / scan / sweep their email inbox
